ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for grilled fish pasta; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest broader class. A more specific term combining fish and pasta is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta, cooked — used as the primary carbohydrate anchor for this mixed pasta dish; no composite grilled-fish-pasta code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for grilled fish pasta. A00GJ (cooked pasta) is used as the closest parent matrix anchor. The fish component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Western pasta dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one plate), comprising roughly 150 g cooked pasta with tomato sauce and 150 g grilled salmon fillet.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Fish Pasta — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database record for Grilled Fish Pasta (cr_id F05060063). All nine reported nutrients are preserved as provided. Fields not reported by HPB (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (white, boiled) GI ~46–50; composite dish estimated at GI 49 based on pasta as dominant carbohydrate matrix
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled fish pasta as a composite dish. GI of 49 is estimated using white pasta (boiled) as the dominant carbohydrate matrix, which has a published GI range of 46–50 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The fish and tomato components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 38.85) / 100 ≈ 18.9, using 12.95 g/100g × 300 g serving = 38.85 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium at 114 mg/100 g; omega-3 fatty acids from salmon support vascular health
hypertension.guidance: With sodium at 114 mg per 100 g (approximately 342 mg per 300 g serving), this dish is well within the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; avoid adding table salt or high-sodium condiments and use fresh tomatoes or low-sodium canned tomatoes in the sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (12.95 g/100 g) with low GI of 49 but limited dietary fibre at 1.34 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of 49 (analogous to boiled white pasta) moderates the glycaemic response, but the relatively low fibre content of 1.34 g/100 g provides limited additional blunting; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider substituting white pasta with wholegrain or legume-based pasta to increase fibre, monitor portion size to keep the carbohydrate load per meal within ADA-recommended targets (45–60 g per meal), and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Oily fish (salmon) provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids; low saturated fat (~0.9 g/100 g); Mediterranean dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled salmon is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular event risk per AHA guidelines; the low saturated fat content (~0.9 g/100 g) and olive oil base align with Mediterranean dietary patterns shown to reduce cardiovascular mortality, making this dish appropriate for cardiovascular disease management when prepared without added salt or cream-based sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from salmon and oleocanthal polyphenols from olive oil provide anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids with established anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; olive oil contributes oleocanthal, a natural COX inhibitor; patients should choose oily fish over white fish and use extra-virgin olive oil to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit, while monitoring individual response to nightshade vegetables (tomatoes) which some RA patients report sensitivity to.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Fish Pasta is a Western-style composite dish consisting of a grilled salmon fillet served alongside or over pasta in a tomato-based sauce. The salmon is typically seasoned and grilled to produce a lightly charred exterior with a moist, flaky interior, delivering a savoury, slightly smoky flavour. The pasta — commonly spaghetti or penne — is cooked al dente and tossed in a tomato sauce prepared with olive oil, garlic, cherry tomatoes, and fresh herbs such as parsley or basil. The dish is popular as a working lunch or casual dinner in Singapore's Western food stalls and cafes. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 140 kcal, 9.36 g protein, 5.31 g fat, and 12.95 g carbohydrate with a low glycaemic index of approximately 49, making it a moderate-carbohydrate, protein-rich meal. Sodium is low at 114 mg/100 g. The salmon component contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is suitable for hypertension management and cardiovascular health due to its low sodium and heart-healthy fat profile. For Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI is beneficial but the limited fibre (1.34 g/100 g) means portion control and wholegrain pasta substitution are advisable. The dish is halal-friendly, dairy-free, and free from added sugars in its standard preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled fish pasta?
Is grilled salmon pasta good for diabetics?
Is fish pasta suitable for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060063, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, white pasta boiled) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate. Saturated fat is estimated at ~0.9 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for grilled salmon and cooked pasta in the proportions described, consistent with the input record's health_suitability notes.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and mineral values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite dish (F05060063) as of 2026. Saturated fat (0.9 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for grilled Atlantic salmon and cooked pasta in approximate proportions consistent with the dish description; this is an adapted estimate and should be verified if precise saturated fat reporting is required. GI of 49 is an analogous estimate based on white pasta (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (49 × 38.85) / 100 ≈ 18.9 g. This is a Western-cuisine dish; no mandatory local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are required under Veritas multilingual rules, which apply to Singaporean dishes. Grilled Fish Pasta is classified as a Western dish served in Singapore and does not carry a distinct Singaporean cultural identity requiring mandatory multilingual naming.
